title: Introduction
text: Welcome to Aura Graph Analytics, Neo4j's cloud-based environment designed for running Graph Data Science workloads. [IMAGE: aga_banner] As you can see, this platform offers a streamlined way to handle complex graph analytics without the need for local installations.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll have the skills to determine when Aura Graph Analytics is the best choice over local GDS installations. You'll learn how to create and manage temporary GDS Sessions using the Python client, project graphs remotely from AuraDB using `gds.graph.project.remote()`, and navigate the Cargo 2000 logistics dataset that we'll explore throughout this module.

title: The Business Question
text: We're diving into five months of air cargo shipping data from a real freight forwarding company. Our mission? To uncover inefficiencies in our routing decisions. Are we choosing the fastest routes? What alternatives have we overlooked? And importantly, how much time and money could we save by optimizing these routes? This module will equip you with the tools to answer these questions using Graph Data Science.

title: Ephemeral compute sessions
text: Instead of setting up GDS on a local database, Aura Graph Analytics allows you to spin up ephemeral compute sessions that connect directly to your data source. [IMAGE: aga_session] Here, you can see an example of an Aura Graph Analytics session in action, providing a flexible and efficient way to handle your data.

title: What you'll learn
text: With Aura Graph Analytics, you'll learn to create and manage GDS Sessions, project logistics networks remotely from AuraDB, and run pathfinding algorithms like Dijkstra and Yen's K-Shortest Paths. You'll also compare historical routes against optimal paths and write results back to the database. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s603-002-new] This flowchart illustrates the steps involved in using Aura Graph Analytics, from session management to running algorithms.

title: Theory vs practical
text: We'll frame each concept using our classic Movies dataset, followed by practical lessons where you'll apply your knowledge to a real-world logistics use-case. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s604-002-existing] Here, you can see a comparison of the graph data models for both the movies and logistics datasets, highlighting different node types and relationships.

title: When to Use Aura Graph Analytics
text: Aura Graph Analytics is particularly useful when you need to run heavy GDS workloads without affecting your production database, scale compute resources independently from storage, work with AuraDB instances that don't have GDS installed locally, or require temporary, cost-efficient analytics environments. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s605-003-new] This diagram illustrates scenarios where Aura Graph Analytics shines, such as separating compute from storage and managing heavy workloads efficiently.

title: GDS Sessions
text: A GDS Session is an ephemeral compute unit for running GDS workloads. There are three types: Attached, where the data source is an AuraDB instance; Self-managed, where the data source is a self-managed Neo4j DBMS; and Standalone, where the data source isn't Neo4j-based, like pandas DataFrames. Sessions automatically expire after inactivity, with a default of 1 hour and a maximum of 7 days.

title: Get your credentials: Dashboard
text: To get started, you'll need to obtain your credentials. Head over to the Aura Dashboard. [IMAGE: aura] Here, you can see the interface where you'll manage your credentials and sessions.

title: Get your credentials: Account Page
text: Next, go to your account page and navigate to the API Keys tab to generate your credentials. [IMAGE: generate_creds] This is where you'll create the keys necessary for accessing Aura Graph Analytics.

title: The GdsSessions Object
text: Once you have the GDS Python client installed, you can create the entry point for session management. Here's a snippet of how you can set up your GdsSessions with your API credentials.

title: Creating a GDS Session
text: To create or reconnect to a session, use the `get_or_create()` method. This allows you to specify session parameters like memory allocation and database connection details. Once set up, you can verify the connection to ensure everything is working smoothly.

title: The `gds` object
text: The `gds` object you receive functions just like the standard `GraphDataScience` client, providing a familiar interface for running your graph algorithms.

title: Remote Projection
text: Projecting a graph into a GDS Session involves using `gds.graph.project()` with a Cypher query that includes `gds.graph.project.remote()`. This allows you to define the structure of your graph and the relationships you want to analyze.

title: Managing Sessions
text: To keep track of your active sessions, you can list them using a simple command. This helps you manage resources and ensure you're not running unnecessary sessions.

title: View your active sessions
text: In your Graph Analytics dashboard, you can easily see which sessions are currently running. [IMAGE: aura_graph_analytics_dashboard] This visual overview helps you manage your sessions effectively.

title: Graph vs Session vs Projection
text: In Aura Graph Analytics, there are three main containers to be aware of: the main graph in AuraDB, the Aura Graph Analytics Session, and the projected graph. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s615-008-new] This diagram illustrates the relationship and differences between these components.

title: Projection workflow
text: Your projection exists within the session, completely separate from the main graph. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s616-006-existing] This diagram shows how the projection workflow operates, emphasizing the independence of the projection from the main graph.

title: Delete a session
text: When you're finished with a session, it's important to delete it to release resources and stop incurring costs. Remember, any data not written back to the database will be lost upon deletion.

title: The Supply Chain Dataset
text: Throughout this module, we'll be working with the Cargo 2000 freight forwarding dataset, which contains real air cargo logistics data from IATA. The graph includes various nodes like `EntryPoint` and `Destination`, and relationships such as `RECEPTION` and `DELIVERY`, each with an `effectiveMinutes` property indicating the time for each process step. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s618-009-new] This structural diagram shows the different node types and relationships within the dataset.

title: Understanding the Data Model
text: The Cargo 2000 dataset is structured into two layers: the Raw Operations Layer, which details every step from `EntryPoint` to `Destination`, and the Historical Summary Layer, which summarizes the routes we've actually shipped. We'll use both layers: the raw operations for finding optimal routes and the historical summaries for comparing against past decisions. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-1-intro-to-aga-s619-010-new] This flowchart depicts the sequence of operations in the raw layer and the summary of routes in the historical layer.

title: Summary
text: Aura Graph Analytics offers on-demand, ephemeral compute for GDS workloads, separating compute from your database and providing automatic session expiration to control costs. It uses the same algorithm API as the standard GDS client. Next, we'll dive into creating sessions and running complete GDS workflows.
